file.content:
# Field Data Capture App — Architecture Analysis

**Date:** 2026-05-22
**Context:** Building a field-facing app that captures inventory / SOP-task / maintenance-task data on-site (QR scan, photos, sensor checks), lets the operator decide whether to push the structured result to WhatsApp or to our own backend, and connects into the central Office Operations intelligence engine.

**Target users:** Low-literacy operators (housekeeping, maintenance, stockroom staff). Low-end Android devices (≤2 GB RAM, Android 8–11, intermittent 3G/4G).

---

# first read the research on inventory Management [Research_on_Inventory_Management.md](./Research_on_Inventory_Management.md)

## 1. Hard requirements vs. what each platform can actually deliver

| Capability | PWA (browser) | TWA (PWA wrapped in Android shell) | React Native / Flutter (native) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (photo) | ✅ `getUserMedia` / `<input capture>` | ✅ | ✅ full control (resolution, flash, focus) |
| QR / barcode scan | ⚠️ via `BarcodeDetector` — **missing on most low-end Androids and iOS Safari**; fallback is a JS lib like `zxing-wasm` which is ~1–3 MB and slow on cheap CPUs | Same as PWA | ✅ ML Kit native — fast, works offline, handles damaged codes |
| GPS / strict geofence | ✅ but the user can deny once and you can't re-prompt cleanly; no background location | ✅ slightly better | ✅ background + foreground, configurable accuracy, mock-location detection |
| Wi-Fi SSID / BSSID check | ❌ **not exposed to browsers** at all. You can only see "online/offline" | ❌ | ✅ can read SSID (with location permission), verify the operator is actually on the site's Wi-Fi |
| Bluetooth (beacons, BLE tags, printers) | ⚠️ Web Bluetooth — **Chrome Android only, not iOS, requires user gesture per device**, no background scan | ⚠️ same | ✅ full BLE stack, beacon ranging, background scan |
| Offline cache | ✅ Service Worker + IndexedDB | ✅ | ✅ SQLite / WatermelonDB / MMKV |
| Offline **queue with retries across reboots** | ⚠️ Background Sync API is Chromium-only and the OS can kill it | ⚠️ same | ✅ WorkManager (Android) guarantees delivery |
| Push notifications | ✅ Android / ❌ iOS Safari is flaky | ✅ Android | ✅ FCM, full reliability |
| App-icon install, no URL bar | ⚠️ "Add to home screen" — users get confused | ✅ looks like an app | ✅ |
| Install size | ~0 (loaded over network first time) | 3–5 MB shell + cache | 15–40 MB |
| First-load on 3G | 2–8 s painful | Same on first run, instant after | Instant (already installed) |
| Update path | Instant, no user action | Instant for web content | Play Store / sideload — users must accept |
| Locking the user into kiosk mode / single app | ❌ | ⚠️ partial | ✅ device-owner mode possible |
| Mock-GPS / rooted-device detection | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Reading the table:** the deal-breakers for a pure PWA are **Wi-Fi SSID verification, reliable BLE, mock-location detection, and guaranteed offline queue delivery**. Each one is explicitly in your requirements.

---

## 2. Recommendation

**Build a native Android app (one codebase via React Native or Flutter), distributed as an APK / private Play track. Treat iOS as out-of-scope for v1 — your user base is on cheap Android.**

Reasons, in priority order:

1. **Wi-Fi SSID check and BLE are non-negotiable** for your "is the operator actually on site" guard. Browsers cannot do this. Anything you build as a PWA will need a native companion anyway — at which point the PWA is dead weight.
2. **Offline queue with guaranteed delivery** matters more for low-literacy users than for power users. A power user will notice "my submission didn't sync" and retry; your users will not. WorkManager + a local SQLite outbox is the only reliable answer.
3. **QR scanning on a ₹6k Android phone** with `zxing-wasm` in a browser is genuinely bad — 1–3 seconds per scan, fails on glare or worn labels. ML Kit native scans in <200 ms and handles damaged codes. For a worker scanning 50 items a shift this is the difference between "works" and "abandoned."
4. **UI affordances for low-literacy users** — big tap targets, voice prompts, haptic feedback on success, locked orientation, no browser chrome stealing screen space, no accidental swipe-to-back closing the app. Native gives you all of this; PWAs leak browser behavior.
5. **Update discipline** — yes, the Play Store update path is slower than PWA, but you can use Play's in-app update API to force critical updates, and you avoid the class of bugs where a stale service worker serves a broken UI for days.

### When a PWA *would* be the right call (not your case)

- Users are office knowledge workers on modern phones.
- No SSID / BLE / background sync requirements.
- You want zero-install adoption.
- You're shipping a v0 prototype and want to test concept before committing.

If you want a 2-week prototype to validate the workflow with one site before building the real thing, a PWA is fine for that throwaway. Do not let the prototype become the product.

---

## 3. Proposed system structure

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Field Android App (RN or Flutter)                            │
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │
│  │ Capture      │  │ Pre-flight   │  │ Routing decision │   │
│  │ • QR (ML Kit)│  │ • GPS fence  │  │ • To WhatsApp    │   │
│  │ • Photo      │  │ • SSID match │  │ • To backend     │   │
│  │ • Form       │  │ • BLE beacon │  │ • To both        │   │
│  │ • Voice note │  │ • Mock-loc?  │  │                  │   │
│  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └────────┬─────────┘   │
│         │                 │                   │             │
│         ▼                 ▼                   ▼             │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ Local SQLite outbox (WatermelonDB)                   │   │
│  │ • capture rows + photo blobs                         │   │
│  │ • status: draft / queued / sent / acked              │   │
│  └────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘   │
│                           │                                 │
│                           ▼                                 │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ WorkManager sync worker (exponential backoff)        │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                             │ HTTPS (multipart for photos)
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Edge ingest service (single endpoint)                       │
│   POST /v1/capture                                          │
│   • validates schema                                        │
│   • stores photo in object storage, returns CDN URL         │
│   • emits event → intelligence engine                       │
│   • fan-out:                                                │
│       ├─ if route=whatsapp → WhatsApp Cloud API template    │
│       ├─ if route=backend  → Office Ops core                │
│       └─ always → audit log + intelligence engine           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Key design choices

**One ingest endpoint, server-side fan-out.** Do *not* let the phone call WhatsApp Cloud API directly. Reasons: WhatsApp tokens would leak onto devices, template approval is centralized, retries and rate-limits need a server. The phone tells the server "route this to WhatsApp" and the server does the WhatsApp call.

**The "route" choice is metadata, not two code paths.** The app sends the same JSON regardless; a `destinations: ["whatsapp", "backend"]` field tells the server where to fan out. This keeps the app dumb and the policy on the server, where you can change it without re-releasing the APK.

**Photos go to object storage, not embedded in JSON.** The app uploads the photo in the same multipart request, the server replaces it with a CDN URL before fan-out. WhatsApp templates support media; passing a URL is cheaper than re-uploading.

**Pre-flight checks run before submit, and their results are signed into the payload.** GPS lat/long, SSID hash, beacon ID, timestamp, device ID — these become tamper-evident evidence on the server side. Don't trust them blindly (a determined user can patch the APK) but log them for audit.

**Intelligence engine subscribes to the event stream, never polls the database.** The ingest service emits a `capture.created` event; the intelligence engine consumes it. This decouples the field app from intelligence-engine availability — if the engine is down, captures still land and get processed when it recovers.

---

## 4. UX considerations for low-literacy users

These are platform-independent but easier to enforce in native:

- **Icons + colors over text.** Green check, red cross, big camera icon. Where text is needed, use the local language and offer audio playback of the prompt.
- **Voice-prompt every screen.** Tap the screen title → it reads aloud. Cheap to add, transformative for adoption.
- **One action per screen.** Don't show a form with 8 fields. Scan QR → confirm item with photo of the label → enter quantity with +/- buttons → done.
- **Haptic + sound on success.** Workers stop trusting silent confirmations.
- **No "submit" word.** Use a big green checkmark button. Use "send" only after the action is irreversible.
- **Show queue state plainly.** A badge "3 waiting to send" on the home screen, not buried in settings. When it clears to 0, celebrate it visibly.
- **Kiosk / single-app lock option** for site-owned devices, so workers can't accidentally exit to a browser or get stuck in a notification.
- **Pre-fill from context.** If the operator scans a QR on a fire extinguisher, the app already knows it's a maintenance task and skips the "what kind of task" question.

---

## 5. Tech stack suggestion

| Layer | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| App framework | **React Native** (or Flutter if the team prefers Dart) | Native modules for ML Kit, BLE, Wi-Fi; one codebase; large hiring pool |
| Local DB | **WatermelonDB** on SQLite | Reactive, scales to 10k+ rows on cheap phones, plays well with sync |
| Sync | **WorkManager** via a native module | Survives reboot, OS-managed backoff |
| QR/Barcode | **ML Kit** (`@react-native-ml-kit/barcode-scanning`) | Free, on-device, fast |
| Photos | `react-native-vision-camera` | Lower-level control than `expo-camera`; needed for compression tuning on cheap devices |
| Geofence | `react-native-geolocation-service` + server-side validation | Don't trust the phone alone |
| BLE | `react-native-ble-plx` | Mature, beacon support |
| Wi-Fi SSID | `react-native-wifi-reborn` | Reads SSID with location permission |
| Push | FCM via `@react-native-firebase/messaging` | Reliable, free |
| Auth | OIDC + short-lived JWT, refresh on Wi-Fi | Don't ship long-lived secrets to the device |
| Distribution | **Private Play track** (preferred) or signed APK + MDM | Auto-updates, rollback |
| Crash / telemetry | Sentry + a custom event for "capture sent" success rate | The metric that matters |

---

## 6. What I would **not** do

- **Don't build a PWA first "to learn" and plan to rewrite.** You will not rewrite. The PWA will accrete features and the day you need SSID checks you'll be stuck.
- **Don't let the phone hit WhatsApp Cloud API directly.** Token sprawl, no rate-limiting, no audit.
- **Don't write your own QR decoder.** ML Kit is free and better than anything you'll ship in a year.
- **Don't make the operator choose "WhatsApp or backend" on every submit.** Choose a sensible default per task type on the server, let them override only when needed.
- **Don't gate submission on connectivity.** Capture first, sync later. Always.
- **Don't try to make iOS work in v1** unless a real customer asks. Every cross-platform compromise is a tax on the 95% case.

---

## 7. Phased rollout

**Phase 1 (4–6 weeks):** Android app with QR scan + photo + form + offline outbox + one ingest endpoint + WhatsApp template fan-out. No BLE, no SSID check yet. Pilot at one site.

**Phase 2 (3–4 weeks):** Add GPS geofence, SSID check, signed pre-flight payloads, audit dashboard. Connect to intelligence engine event stream.

**Phase 3 (4 weeks):** BLE beacons for indoor location, voice prompts, kiosk mode for site-owned devices, in-app updates.

**Phase 4:** iOS if and only if customer demand justifies it.

---

## 8. Open questions to resolve before coding

1. **Who owns the device?** Site-owned (kiosk mode possible, you can pre-provision) vs. operator-owned (BYOD, harder UX, training cost). Changes the answer for distribution and security.
2. **WhatsApp account model.** One business number per site, or one central number with site identifier in the template? Affects template approval count and reply routing.
3. **What happens to a capture sent to WhatsApp — does anyone *act* on it, or is it informational?** If actionable, the WhatsApp reply needs to flow back into the intelligence engine, which means you need WhatsApp webhook handling on the server.
4. **Photo retention policy.** Cheap phones fill up fast. Local cache must auto-evict after server-ack.
5. **Language coverage.** Hindi + English minimum? Regional languages? Voice prompts must be pre-recorded per language.
6. **What's the failure UX when a pre-flight check fails?** ("You are not on site Wi-Fi" — can they override with a supervisor PIN? Or hard block?)

Answer these six before locking the spec.

the barcodes we have behind the product like packed groceries, what are the used for? 

The barcodes behind products like groceries are mainly used for identification, tracking, billing, and inventory management across the entire supply chain.

The most common one you see is called a UPC or EAN barcode.

Here is what happens behind the scenes:

1. Product Identification

Each barcode represents a unique product ID.

For example:

A packet of Oreo biscuits 100g might have:
8901234567890

That number identifies:

1. Brand
2. Product type
3. Variant
4. Size
5. Manufacturer

So when scanned, the system instantly knows:
“this is Oreo vanilla cream 100g”

Not just “biscuits”.

Think of it like a digital fingerprint for products.

⸻

2. Billing at Checkout

At supermarkets, the cashier scans the barcode using a laser or camera scanner.

The POS system then:

1. Finds the product in the database
2. Fetches:
    1. Name
    2. Price
    3. Tax
    4. Discounts
3. Adds it to the bill instantly

Without barcode systems:

1. Staff would manually type product names
2. Billing would be slower
3. Mistakes would increase

⸻

3. Inventory Management

This is one of the biggest uses.

Every scan updates stock count automatically.

Example flow:

1. Warehouse receives 500 Maggi packets
2. System records inventory
3. Products go to shelves
4. Customers buy 120 packets
5. Barcode scans reduce stock count automatically
6. System alerts when stock becomes low

Large companies heavily depend on this.

This is how:

1. Amazon
2. Dmart
3. Reliance Fresh
4. Walmart

track millions of products accurately.

⸻

4. Supply Chain Tracking

Barcodes help track products across:

1. Factory
2. Warehouse
3. Trucks
4. Stores
5. Customers

This enables:

1. Shipment tracking
2. Batch tracking
3. Recall management
4. Expiry management

Example:
If a milk batch becomes contaminated, the company can identify exactly which stores received that batch.

⸻

5. Faster Warehousing

Workers use handheld barcode scanners.

They scan:

1. Incoming products
2. Shelf locations
3. Shipment boxes

This helps:

1. Reduce human error
2. Increase speed
3. Automate logistics

Modern warehouses combine:

1. Barcodes
2. RFID
3. AI systems
4. Robotics

⸻

6. Product Information Lookup

Sometimes the barcode itself only stores an ID.

The actual information is stored in a database.

So the flow is:

Barcode → Product ID → Database lookup → Full product data

Exactly like:
User ID → Database → User profile

As a developer, you can think of barcode as a primary key.

⸻

Types of Product Codes

UPC

Mostly used in the US.

EAN

International standard.
Very common in India.

QR Code

Stores much more data directly.
Can contain:

1. URLs
2. Payment data
3. Contact info
4. Authentication tokens

RFID Tags

Advanced version using radio waves.
No direct line of sight needed.

You were already exploring this world with your RC522 and NFC setup. NFC is actually a specialized subset of RFID.

⸻

How Barcode Scanners Work

Older scanners:

1. Emit laser light
2. Read reflected patterns

Black bars absorb light.
White spaces reflect light.

The scanner converts this into binary data.

Modern scanners:

1. Use cameras
2. Decode via image processing

Phones can do this too.

⸻

Interesting Engineering Insight

A barcode itself usually does NOT contain:

1. Price
2. Product name
3. Inventory count

It mainly contains an identifier.

The intelligence comes from:

1. Databases
2. POS software
3. Inventory systems
4. ERP systems

The barcode is just the bridge between physical and digital worlds.

That simple set of black lines is actually part of a massive global synchronization system connecting:

1. Manufacturers
2. Warehouses
3. Retailers
4. Logistics
5. Finance systems
6. Consumers

It is one of the most successful low cost engineering standards ever created.

---

like how does the barcode is created, like I just see numbers when I scan the barcode, are they just UID or combination of information?

Yes. In most grocery products, the barcode is mainly a structured unique identifier, not the full product information itself.

The numbers are not random. They follow global standards.

For example, a typical Indian grocery barcode may look like:

8901234567890

This is usually an EAN 13 barcode.

Let’s break it down.

Structure of a Barcode Number

Example: EAN 13

Format:

XXX XXXXX XXXXX C

Where:

1. Prefix / Country code
2. Company identifier
3. Product identifier
4. Check digit

⸻

1. Country / GS1 Prefix

Example:
890

This indicates the barcode was registered in India.

Some common prefixes:

1. 890 → India
2. 000 to 139 → USA/Canada
3. 690 to 699 → China

Important:
This does NOT always mean the product was manufactured there.
It mainly means the barcode was registered there.

⸻

2. Company Identifier

Example:
12345

Assigned by an organization called GS1.

This identifies the company.

Think of it like:
Company namespace.

⸻

3. Product Identifier

Example:
67890

Chosen by the company itself.

This differentiates:

1. Flavors
2. Sizes
3. Variants
4. Packaging

For example:

1. Coke 250ml
2. Coke 500ml
3. Diet Coke

All get different product IDs.

⸻

4. Check Digit

Final digit:
0

This is used for error detection.

The scanner mathematically verifies:
“Was this barcode scanned correctly?”

If the computed check digit mismatches:

1. Scan fails
2. System retries

This prevents many reading errors.

⸻

So What Does the Barcode Actually Store?

Usually:
Just the numeric identifier.

Example:
8901234567890

The POS system then does:

Scan barcode
   ↓
Get number
   ↓
Search database
   ↓
Retrieve:
    Name
    Price
    Tax
    Inventory
    Expiry

So the barcode is more like:
A database key.

Very similar to:

SELECT * FROM products WHERE barcode='8901234567890';

⸻

How Are Black Lines Generated?

This is where it gets interesting.

The numbers are converted into patterns of:

1. Black bars
2. White spaces

Each digit has a binary-like visual encoding.

Example idea:

Digit 7 may become something like:

1010001

Which becomes:

Black White Black White White White Black

The scanner reads widths and spacing.

So the barcode is essentially:
Visual binary encoding.

⸻

Important Insight

The bars themselves are the real data.

The numbers printed below are mainly for humans.

Even if the printed numbers disappear:
A scanner can still read the bars.

⸻

Who Creates Barcodes?

Companies register with GS1.

Then they receive:

1. Company prefix
2. Barcode ranges

Then they generate product barcodes.

⸻

Can You Create Your Own Barcode?

Technically yes.

You can generate barcode images easily using libraries.

Example in JavaScript:

JsBarcode("#barcode", "8901234567890");

Or Python:

from barcode import EAN13

But unofficial barcodes will not work properly in large retail ecosystems unless registered.

⸻

Advanced Types

QR Codes

Unlike normal barcodes:
QR codes can directly store:

1. URLs
2. Text
3. JSON
4. Payment info

A normal barcode is mostly:
Identifier only.

A QR code is more like:
Portable storage.

⸻

RFID vs Barcode

You were exploring NFC/RFID already, so here is the important distinction:

Feature	Barcode	RFID/NFC
Needs line of sight	Yes	No
Stores data	Small	More
Read distance	Very short	Medium
Cost	Very cheap	More expensive
Scan multiple at once	No	Yes

That is why grocery stores mostly use barcodes:
Extremely cheap and scalable.

A printed barcode costs almost nothing.

---

so barcodes of a product change by batch? 

Usually, no.

The barcode of a product generally stays the same across batches.

Example:

Every 500g packet of a specific biscuit variant may use the same barcode for years.

What changes between batches is usually:

1. Manufacturing date
2. Expiry date
3. Batch/Lot number

Those are often printed separately from the main barcode.

So the flow is usually:

Thing	Changes per batch?
Product barcode	No
Batch/Lot number	Yes
Manufacturing date	Yes
Expiry date	Yes

⸻

Why Keep Barcode Same?

Because the barcode identifies the product type, not the individual item.

Example:

All these may share the same barcode:

1. Maggi Masala 70g packet produced in January
2. Same product produced in February
3. Same product produced in March

Because for billing and inventory:
Store only needs:
“this is Maggi 70g”

Not:
“this specific packet from factory line 3 at 11:42 AM”

⸻

Then How Are Batches Tracked?

Using:

1. Batch number
2. Lot number
3. Sometimes secondary barcodes or QR codes

Example:

Barcode: 8901234567890
Batch: B240526A
MFG: 25/05/2026
EXP: 25/11/2026

⸻

Why Batch Numbers Matter

They are critical for:

1. Product Recall

Suppose contamination happens in:
Batch B240526A

Company can recall only affected packets instead of all products.

Very important in:

1. Food
2. Medicine
3. Cosmetics

⸻

2. Expiry Tracking

Warehouses track:

1. Which batch arrived first
2. Which expires sooner

This enables:
FIFO or FEFO inventory systems.

FIFO:
First In First Out

FEFO:
First Expiry First Out

⸻

3. Quality Control

If customers complain:
“this batch tastes weird”

The company can:

1. Identify production line
2. Check raw materials
3. Trace factory conditions

⸻

Do Some Systems Use Unique Codes Per Item?

Yes.

Especially in:

1. Luxury products
2. Electronics
3. Pharmaceuticals
4. High value logistics

They may use:

1. QR codes
2. Serialized barcodes
3. RFID tags

Where every single item gets a unique identity.

Example:
iPhones have unique serial numbers.

⸻

In Pharmacies and Advanced Retail

You may see:

Main Barcode

Identifies product type.

Secondary Code

Contains:

1. Batch
2. Expiry
3. Serial number

Often using:

1. QR
2. DataMatrix
3. GS1 2D codes

⸻

Interesting Technical Insight

Traditional barcodes are optimized for:

1. Speed
2. Cheap printing
3. Universal compatibility

That is why they intentionally keep data minimal.

The real intelligence is stored in:

1. ERP systems
2. Warehouse databases
3. POS systems
4. Supply chain software

Modern systems are increasingly shifting toward:

1. QR based traceability
2. RFID inventory
3. Real time digital twins of inventory

Especially after e commerce and global logistics became massive.

---

how barcode are registered in mallsstores  and warehouse, like someone has to manually map the product info with barcode number?

Yes. Somewhere in the system, product information must be mapped to the barcode number. But in modern retail, much of this is standardized and automated.

The process is different for:

1. Small shops
2. Supermarkets
3. Large retail chains
4. Warehouses
5. Manufacturers

High Level Flow

Manufacturer creates barcode
        ↓
Product enters distributor database
        ↓
Retailer imports product catalog
        ↓
POS/ERP maps barcode → product info
        ↓
Scanner reads barcode
        ↓
System fetches product details

⸻

Scenario 1: Small Shop

In a local shop, yes, someone may manually map products.

Example:

Barcode: 8901234567890
Name: Maggi 70g
Price: ₹14
Stock: 120

The shopkeeper may:

1. Scan product once
2. Enter details manually
3. Save into POS software

Next time:
Scanner automatically recognizes it.

This is common in:

1. Kirana stores
2. Small pharmacies
3. Small boutiques

⸻

Scenario 2: Supermarkets & Malls

Large stores usually do NOT manually enter every product one by one.

Instead they receive:

1. Supplier catalogs
2. CSV files
3. ERP integrations
4. Vendor databases

Example:

A distributor sends:

barcode,name,price,category
8901234567890,Maggi 70g,14,Noodles
8901234567891,Coke 500ml,40,Beverage

The mall imports this into:

1. POS
2. ERP
3. Inventory system

Now all billing counters recognize products instantly.

⸻

Scenario 3: Warehouses

Warehouses care more about:

1. Storage location
2. Quantity
3. Movement
4. Batch tracking

Their systems map:

Barcode → SKU → Warehouse location

Example:

8901234567890
   ↓
SKU: MAGGI70
   ↓
Rack A3 Shelf 5

Workers scan:

1. Product barcode
2. Shelf barcode

System updates inventory location.

⸻

What Is SKU?

SKU = Stock Keeping Unit

Internal company identifier.

Important distinction:

Thing	Purpose
Barcode	Universal product identity
SKU	Internal business identity

Example:

Barcode: 8901234567890
SKU: MAG-NDL-70-MAS

Companies often use their own SKU system internally.

⸻

How Stores Initially Get Product Data

Method 1: Manual Entry

Small scale.

Method 2: Supplier Feed

Distributor sends product catalog.

Method 3: ERP Integration

Large chains integrate directly with suppliers.

Method 4: Centralized Retail Database

Corporate HQ manages all products centrally.

Example:
Walmart stores don’t manually add products individually at every branch.

Corporate systems distribute product master data across all stores.

⸻

What Happens During Billing?

When scanner reads:

8901234567890

POS does:

SELECT * FROM inventory
WHERE barcode='8901234567890';

Returns:

{
  "name": "Maggi 70g",
  "price": 14,
  "tax": "5%",
  "stock": 120
}

Then:

1. Bill updates
2. Stock reduces
3. Sales analytics update

All in milliseconds.

⸻

In Large Warehouses

Modern warehouses may use:

1. Barcode scanners
2. RFID gates
3. Conveyor scanners
4. AI cameras
5. Autonomous robots

Systems commonly involved:

1. WMS
    Warehouse Management System
2. ERP
    Enterprise Resource Planning
3. OMS
    Order Management System
4. POS
    Point of Sale

⸻

Interesting Real World Insight

A huge engineering challenge is not barcode scanning.

It is maintaining accurate product master data.

Problems include:

1. Duplicate products
2. Wrong pricing
3. Mismatched barcodes
4. Supplier inconsistencies
5. Unit conversion issues

Example:
One supplier writes:
“Coca Cola 500ML”

Another:
“Coke 0.5L”

Another:
“CocaCola Half Liter”

Large companies spend massive effort normalizing this data.

⸻

This Is Why GS1 Exists

GS1 helps standardize:

1. Product identifiers
2. Company prefixes
3. Logistics codes
4. Supply chain interoperability

Without standards:
Global retail would become chaos.

⸻

As a Developer

You can think of the whole retail system as:

Physical product
      ↕
Barcode
      ↕
Database record
      ↕
Business logic

The barcode is simply the bridge connecting real world objects to software systems.

That simple scan triggers:

1. Inventory update
2. Financial transaction
3. Analytics event
4. Supply chain update
5. Tax calculation
6. Demand forecasting
7. Reordering logic

All from a few black lines printed on plastic.

---

what tools for inventory management are present in the market today, and what would be the cost if I use them to maintain our office cafeteria? 

For an office cafeteria, you do NOT need enterprise warehouse software initially.

Your requirements are probably:

1. Ingredient inventory tracking
2. Grocery stock management
3. Daily consumption tracking
4. Billing or employee meal tracking
5. Low stock alerts
6. Expiry monitoring
7. Vendor purchase tracking
8. Basic analytics

That is a small to medium scale inventory + cafeteria management problem.

⸻

What Tools Exist Today?

These are the most common categories:

Type	Used By	Complexity	Cost
Excel/Google Sheets	Small offices	Very low	Free
POS + Inventory software	Cafes/restaurants	Medium	₹500 to ₹6000/month
ERP systems	Large companies	High	₹50k to lakhs
Custom software	Organizations with special workflows	Variable	Depends

⸻

Best Practical Options For Office Cafeteria

1.  Zoho Inventory￼

Very good for:

1. Stock management
2. Purchase tracking
3. Vendor management
4. Barcode support
5. Reports

Pricing starts around:
₹2000 to ₹5000/month for SMEs.  ￼

Good if:

1. Your cafeteria already has structured procurement
2. Multiple vendors
3. Need professional reporting

⸻

2.  Petpooja￼

Very common in Indian restaurants and cafeterias.

Features:

1. Billing
2. Inventory
3. Recipe management
4. Kitchen orders
5. Consumption deduction
6. GST billing

Pricing:
Typically ₹1500 to ₹6000/month depending on features.  ￼

Very suitable for:

1. Cafeterias
2. Food counters
3. Internal food operations

⸻

3.  POSist by Restroworks￼

More enterprise oriented.

Used by:

1. Restaurant chains
2. Corporate cafeterias
3. Multi outlet operations

Features:

1. Inventory
2. Recipe level stock deduction
3. Analytics
4. Wastage tracking
5. Vendor management

Cost:
Usually ₹4000 to ₹15000/month+ depending on scale.  ￼

⸻

4.  LimeTray Inventory Management￼

Good for:

1. Food inventory
2. Raw material tracking
3. Alerts
4. Food wastage optimization

They claim restaurants can reduce wastage significantly.  ￼

⸻

5.  GOFRUGAL POS￼

Popular in India.

Good balance between:

1. POS
2. Inventory
3. Kitchen management
4. Billing

Often used in:

1. Retail stores
2. Cafes
3. Small restaurants

⸻

What Hardware Would You Need?

For a cafeteria:

Minimal Setup

Hardware	Approx Cost
Barcode scanner	₹1500 to ₹4000
Thermal bill printer	₹4000 to ₹10000
Android tablet/PC	Existing device works
Barcode labels	Optional
POS terminal	Optional

⸻

Realistically, What Should YOU Use?

Based on your profile and engineering mindset, here are the smartest paths:

Option A: Ultra Lean

Use:

1. Google Sheets
2. QR/barcode scanning app
3. Simple Node.js dashboard

Cost:
Almost free.

Good if:

1. Cafeteria is small
2. You want learning + experimentation
3. Internal use only

You could literally build this yourself.

And honestly, for many office cafeterias:
A custom lightweight system is often better than bloated ERP software.

⸻

Option B: Practical Professional Setup

Use:

1. Petpooja
2. Zoho Inventory
3. Barcode scanner

Cost:
₹2000 to ₹8000/month total.

This is probably the sweet spot.

⸻

Option C: Enterprise Grade

Use:

1. POSist
2. Oracle Simphony
3. ERP integrations

Cost:
₹50k to lakhs/year.  ￼

Overkill for most office cafeterias.

⸻

Important Insight

Inventory systems for cafeterias are MUCH more complicated than grocery stores because of recipe consumption.

Example:

Selling:
1 Veg sandwich

Must automatically deduct:

1. Bread slices
2. Butter
3. Tomato
4. Onion
5. Cheese
6. Sauce

This is called:
Recipe mapping or BOM
Bill of Materials.

That is where restaurant inventory systems become powerful.

⸻

What Would I Personally Recommend For Your Scenario?

Considering your technical skills:

You could build an extremely powerful internal cafeteria system using:

1. Next.js
2. PostgreSQL
3. Barcode scanner input
4. QR employee auth
5. Recipe based inventory deduction
6. Analytics dashboard
7. Vendor management
8. AI based consumption forecasting later

This is actually a very strong real world systems engineering project.

It combines:

1. Inventory systems
2. Realtime updates
3. Authentication
4. Analytics
5. ERP concepts
6. Supply chain logic
7. Database optimization
8. Human workflow engineering

Which is exactly the kind of thing companies pay serious engineers to solve.

# PWA Prototype vs React Native: Decision for Inventory Field App

## Your Specific Workflows (Mapped Out)

Before deciding the platform, let's be precise about what you're actually building:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["📋 DMart Bill / Receipt\n(photo via Slack / Telegram / Dashboard)"] -->|"Server-side AI extraction"| B["Extract line items:\nproduct, qty, price"]
    B --> C["Update Orders List\n(expected deliveries)"]
  
    C -.->|"Synced to field app"| D["🚪 Gate Entry\n(Guard scans items arriving)"]
    D --> E{"Barcode known?"}
  
    E -->|Yes| F["Auto-identify product\n+ show expected qty"]
    E -->|No| G["📝 Map barcode → product\n(one-time registration)"]
    G --> F
  
    F --> H{"Qty matches order?"}
    H -->|"✅ Match"| I["Mark as received\nUpdate inventory"]
    H -->|"❌ Mismatch"| J["Flag discrepancy\nAlert admin"]
  
    I --> K["🔮 Future: Proof Photo\n(AI verification)"]
    J --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Bill photos are NOT captured from the field app.** They arrive through existing channels (Slack, Telegram, admin dashboard) and are processed server-side by the intelligence engine. The field app's only job is **gate scanning + product mapping**.

### The 5 Core Capabilities You Need

| #   | Capability                               | Where it runs                                               | What it requires                                                       | PWA can do it?                                      | Native can do it?                         |
| --- | ---------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
| 1   | **Extract bill data from DMart receipt** | **Server-side** (bill arrives via Slack/Telegram/Dashboard) | AI OCR extraction pipeline                                             | N/A — not a field app concern                       | N/A — not a field app concern             |
| 2   | **Barcode scanning at gate**             | **Field app**                                               | Fast, reliable barcode reader. Guard scanning 20-50 items per delivery | ⚠️**Slow on cheap phones** (~1-3s/scan with JS libs) | ✅ ML Kit: <200ms, handles glare           |
| 3   | **Barcode ↔ product mapping**            | **Field app**                                               | Database lookup + registration form                                    | ✅ Works fine                                        | ✅ Works fine                              |
| 4   | **Quantity validation**                  | **Field app** (against server-synced orders)                | Compare scanned count vs order                                         | ✅ Works fine                                        | ✅ Works fine                              |
| 5   | **Proof photo (future)**                 | **Field app**                                               | High-quality photo with metadata, tamper-evidence                      | ⚠️ Limited camera control                            | ✅ Full control: resolution, EXIF, signing |

---

## My Recommendation: **Skip the PWA. Build React Native directly.**

Here's why this is different from the usual "prototype first" advice:

### Reason 1: Barcode scanning is the central interaction — and it's the PWA's biggest weakness

Your guard is standing at a gate, scanning items as they come off a truck. This is **not** a "scan one QR occasionally" use case. This is **rapid sequential scanning** — 20-50 items per delivery, potentially multiple deliveries per day.

On a ₹6-8K Android phone (which is what your guard has):

- **PWA with zxing-wasm**: 1-3 seconds per scan, fails on damaged/worn barcodes, struggles with glare
- **Native ML Kit**: <200ms per scan, handles damaged codes, works in poor lighting

At 30 items per delivery, that's the difference between **1 minute** (native) and **1.5 minutes with frustrating failures** (PWA). The guard will abandon the app after day 2.

### Reason 2: The "throwaway prototype" never gets thrown away

Your own architecture doc says it best:

> Don't build a PWA first "to learn" and plan to rewrite. You will not rewrite. The PWA will accrete features and the day you need SSID checks you'll be stuck.

This is especially true for you because the barcode-product mapping database you build during the PWA phase **is the valuable data**. Once you have 200 products mapped, migrating that AND retraining staff on a new app is more expensive than building native from day 1.

### Reason 3: The workflows you described are the MVP — they're not complex enough to need "concept validation"

Let me be blunt: the concept is already validated. You know:

- Guards need to scan incoming items ✅
- Bills need to be digitized ✅
- Barcodes need to map to products ✅
- Quantities need validation ✅

There's no product-market-fit risk here. This is an internal operations tool for a known workflow. You don't need a PWA to "test if people will use it" — they'll use it because it's their job and you'll mandate it.

### Reason 4: The offline queue matters for gate entry

When a truck arrives at 7 AM and the internet is spotty, the guard **must** be able to scan everything and sync later. Your architecture doc correctly identifies that:

- PWA `Background Sync` is Chromium-only and the OS can kill it
- Native `WorkManager` guarantees delivery across reboots

A half-synced delivery is worse than a paper log.

---

## What Would Change My Mind (When a PWA IS Right)

A PWA would make sense if:

- ❌ Your primary users were office staff on modern phones (not guards on cheap Androids)
- ❌ Scanning was occasional, not the core workflow
- ❌ You had genuine uncertainty about whether the workflow would work at all
- ❌ You needed zero-install adoption across many untrained users

None of these apply to your case.

---

## Recommended Approach: React Native, Phased Build

### Phase 1 — Field App: Core Scanner + Mapping (3-4 weeks)

Build the minimum that makes gate entry digital:

| Feature              | Details                                                                       |
| -------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Barcode scanner**  | ML Kit via `@react-native-ml-kit/barcode-scanning`                            |
| **Product registry** | Scan barcode → if unknown, register (name, category, unit) → save to local DB |
| **Gate entry flow**  | Guard scans items → app shows product info → guard confirms quantity          |
| **Offline outbox**   | WatermelonDB + WorkManager. Everything works without internet.                |
| **Order list sync**  | Orders created server-side (from bill extraction) sync down to the field app  |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Start with the **barcode → product mapping** flow. This is your data flywheel. Every scan enriches your product database. Within 2-3 weeks of real use, most common items will be registered.

### Phase 2 — Server-side: Bill Extraction Pipeline (2-3 weeks, parallel track)

This runs **entirely on the backend** — no field app changes needed:

| Feature                      | Where     | Details                                                                          |
| ---------------------------- | --------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Bill photo ingestion**     | Server    | Receive bill photos from Slack, Telegram, or admin dashboard                     |
| **AI bill extraction**       | Server    | Photo → Claude/Gemini OCR → structured line items (product, qty, price)          |
| **Order creation**           | Server    | Extracted bill data auto-creates "expected delivery" entries                     |
| **Quantity mismatch alerts** | Server    | Compare gate scan totals (from field app) vs bill line items, flag discrepancies |
| **Admin review UI**          | Dashboard | Admin reviews/corrects AI-extracted data before confirming order                 |

> [!TIP]
> Phase 1 and Phase 2 can run **in parallel**. The field app works standalone with manually-entered orders while the bill extraction pipeline is being built. Once Phase 2 is live, orders auto-populate from bills.

### Phase 3 — Intelligence + Proof (3-4 weeks, future)

| Feature                 | Where                   | Details                                                                       |
| ----------------------- | ----------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Proof photos**        | Field app               | Photo of received goods, timestamped, GPS-tagged, stored with delivery record |
| **AI verification**     | Server                  | Compare proof photo contents against expected items                           |
| **Analytics dashboard** | Dashboard               | Delivery accuracy rates, vendor reliability scores, consumption patterns      |
| **Reorder alerts**      | Server → Slack/Telegram | Trigger when stock drops below threshold                                      |

---

## The Barcode Mapping Flow (Your Most Important Data Asset)

This deserves special attention because it's the foundation of everything:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ FIRST SCAN (barcode unknown)                            │
│                                                          │
│  Guard scans: 8901234567890                              │
│       ↓                                                  │
│  App: "New product! What is this?"                       │
│       ↓                                                  │
│  Guard sees: [📷 Photo of product]                       │
│              [Name: ____________]   ← big tap keyboard   │
│              [Category: ▼ Grocery / Cleaning / Office]   │
│              [Unit: ▼ Piece / Kg / Litre / Pack]         │
│       ↓                                                  │
│  Saved: barcode 8901234567890 = "Tata Salt 1kg"          │
│                                                          │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ EVERY SUBSEQUENT SCAN                                    │
│                                                          │
│  Guard scans: 8901234567890                              │
│       ↓                                                  │
│  App instantly shows:                                    │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                    │
│  │  🧂 Tata Salt 1kg               │                    │
│  │  Expected: 10 units (from order) │                    │
│  │  Scanned so far: 7              │                    │
│  │                                  │                    │
│  │  [+1] [+5] [Custom qty]          │                    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Accelerator idea**: For the first batch of products, instead of making the guard register each one, extract the product list from DMart bills first. The AI extracts `{name, qty, price}` from the receipt — you pre-populate the product registry. When the guard scans at the gate, most items are already known.

---

## Open Questions for You

1. **Who is the guard?** Is it a dedicated security person, or a rotating staff member? This affects training investment and whether you can use device-owner/kiosk mode.
2. **How many unique products do you typically receive?** If it's 50-100 recurring items, the barcode mapping database fills up in 1-2 weeks. If it's 500+, you may want to consider pre-loading from a public barcode database (like Open Food Facts for grocery items).
3. **DMart bills — are they always printed (thermal receipt), or sometimes handwritten?** This affects whether the server-side AI extraction needs general OCR or can use structured receipt parsing.
4. **Which channels will bill photos come from most often?** Slack, Telegram, admin dashboard upload — or all three? This determines how many ingestion integrations the backend pipeline needs.
5. **Should the AI auto-create orders from bills, or should an admin review/approve first?** Auto-create is faster but risks bad OCR creating wrong orders. Admin review adds a human checkpoint but adds latency.
6. **Is quantity validation per-item (scan each individual item) or per-line-item (scan one, enter count)?** Per-item is more accurate but slower; per-line-item with manual count entry is faster but relies on the guard counting correctly.

---

## Bottom Line

| Path                      | Verdict                                                                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PWA prototype first**   | ❌ Don't do this. Your core workflow (rapid barcode scanning) is the exact thing PWAs are worst at. You'll build it, hate the scanning, then build native anyway. |
| **React Native directly** | ✅**Do this.** The workflows are well-understood, the use case is internal (no product-market-fit risk), and the barcode scanning quality gap is a dealbreaker.   |

Start with Phase 1 (scanner + mapping). You can have a guard scanning items at the gate within 3-4 weeks. The bill extraction and AI features layer on top of a working foundation.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- bifrost-platform: Use this skill when an agent needs to deploy applications, manage infrastructure, authenticate, or troubleshoot delivery workflows through the Bifrost CLI. It teaches the agent to use the native `bifrost` binary directly, run a strong preflight for repo/app/platform context, prefer `git` before `gh`, pick sensible project/service defaults, and finish with deployment URLs and actionable diagnostics.

1	# Field Data Capture App — Architecture Analysis
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-05-22
4	**Context:** Building a field-facing app that captures inventory / SOP-task / maintenance-task data on-site (QR scan, photos, sensor checks), lets the operator decide whether to push the structured result to WhatsApp or to our own backend, and connects into the central Office Operations intelligence engine.
5	
6	**Target users:** Low-literacy operators (housekeeping, maintenance, stockroom staff). Low-end Android devices (≤2 GB RAM, Android 8–11, intermittent 3G/4G).
7	
8	---
9	
10	# first read the research on inventory Management [Research_on_Inventory_Management.md](./Research_on_Inventory_Management.md)
11	
12	## 1. Hard requirements vs. what each platform can actually deliver
13	
14	| Capability | PWA (browser) | TWA (PWA wrapped in Android shell) | React Native / Flutter (native) |
15	|---|---|---|---|
16	| Camera (photo) | ✅ `getUserMedia` / `<input capture>` | ✅ | ✅ full control (resolution, flash, focus) |
17	| QR / barcode scan | ⚠️ via `BarcodeDetector` — **missing on most low-end Androids and iOS Safari**; fallback is a JS lib like `zxing-wasm` which is ~1–3 MB and slow on cheap CPUs | Same as PWA | ✅ ML Kit native — fast, works offline, handles damaged codes |
18	| GPS / strict geofence | ✅ but the user can deny once and you can't re-prompt cleanly; no background location | ✅ slightly better | ✅ background + foreground, configurable accuracy, mock-location detection |
19	| Wi-Fi SSID / BSSID check | ❌ **not exposed to browsers** at all. You can only see "online/offline" | ❌ | ✅ can read SSID (with location permission), verify the operator is actually on the site's Wi-Fi |
20	| Bluetooth (beacons, BLE tags, printers) | ⚠️ Web Bluetooth — **Chrome Android only, not iOS, requires user gesture per device**, no background scan | ⚠️ same | ✅ full BLE stack, beacon ranging, background scan |
21	| Offline cache | ✅ Service Worker + IndexedDB | ✅ | ✅ SQLite / WatermelonDB / MMKV |
22	| Offline **queue with retries across reboots** | ⚠️ Background Sync API is Chromium-only and the OS can kill it | ⚠️ same | ✅ WorkManager (Android) guarantees delivery |
23	| Push notifications | ✅ Android / ❌ iOS Safari is flaky | ✅ Android | ✅ FCM, full reliability |
24	| App-icon install, no URL bar | ⚠️ "Add to home screen" — users get confused | ✅ looks like an app | ✅ |
25	| Install size | ~0 (loaded over network first time) | 3–5 MB shell + cache | 15–40 MB |
26	| First-load on 3G | 2–8 s painful | Same on first run, instant after | Instant (already installed) |
27	| Update path | Instant, no user action | Instant for web content | Play Store / sideload — users must accept |
28	| Locking the user into kiosk mode / single app | ❌ | ⚠️ partial | ✅ device-owner mode possible |
29	| Mock-GPS / rooted-device detection | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
30	
31	**Reading the table:** the deal-breakers for a pure PWA are **Wi-Fi SSID verification, reliable BLE, mock-location detection, and guaranteed offline queue delivery**. Each one is explicitly in your requirements.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## 2. Recommendation
36	
37	**Build a native Android app (one codebase via React Native or Flutter), distributed as an APK / private Play track. Treat iOS as out-of-scope for v1 — your user base is on cheap Android.**
38	
39	Reasons, in priority order:
40	
41	1. **Wi-Fi SSID check and BLE are non-negotiable** for your "is the operator actually on site" guard. Browsers cannot do this. Anything you build as a PWA will need a native companion anyway — at which point the PWA is dead weight.
42	2. **Offline queue with guaranteed delivery** matters more for low-literacy users than for power users. A power user will notice "my submission didn't sync" and retry; your users will not. WorkManager + a local SQLite outbox is the only reliable answer.
43	3. **QR scanning on a ₹6k Android phone** with `zxing-wasm` in a browser is genuinely bad — 1–3 seconds per scan, fails on glare or worn labels. ML Kit native scans in <200 ms and handles damaged codes. For a worker scanning 50 items a shift this is the difference between "works" and "abandoned."
44	4. **UI affordances for low-literacy users** — big tap targets, voice prompts, haptic feedback on success, locked orientation, no browser chrome stealing screen space, no accidental swipe-to-back closing the app. Native gives you all of this; PWAs leak browser behavior.
45	5. **Update discipline** — yes, the Play Store update path is slower than PWA, but you can use Play's in-app update API to force critical updates, and you avoid the class of bugs where a stale service worker serves a broken UI for days.
46	
47	### When a PWA *would* be the right call (not your case)
48	
49	- Users are office knowledge workers on modern phones.
50	- No SSID / BLE / background sync requirements.
51	- You want zero-install adoption.
52	- You're shipping a v0 prototype and want to test concept before committing.
53	
54	If you want a 2-week prototype to validate the workflow with one site before building the real thing, a PWA is fine for that throwaway. Do not let the prototype become the product.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## 3. Proposed system structure
59	
60	```
61	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
62	│ Field Android App (RN or Flutter)                            │
63	│                                                              │
64	│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐   │
65	│  │ Capture      │  │ Pre-flight   │  │ Routing decision │   │
66	│  │ • QR (ML Kit)│  │ • GPS fence  │  │ • To WhatsApp    │   │
67	│  │ • Photo      │  │ • SSID match │  │ • To backend     │   │
68	│  │ • Form       │  │ • BLE beacon │  │ • To both        │   │
69	│  │ • Voice note │  │ • Mock-loc?  │  │                  │   │
70	│  └──────┬───────┘  └──────┬───────┘  └────────┬─────────┘   │
71	│         │                 │                   │             │
72	│         ▼                 ▼                   ▼             │
73	│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
74	│  │ Local SQLite outbox (WatermelonDB)                   │   │
75	│  │ • capture rows + photo blobs                         │   │
76	│  │ • status: draft / queued / sent / acked              │   │
77	│  └────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────┘   │
78	│                           │                                 │
79	│                           ▼                                 │
80	│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
81	│  │ WorkManager sync worker (exponential backoff)        │   │
82	│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
83	└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
84	                             │ HTTPS (multipart for photos)
85	                             ▼
86	┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
87	│ Edge ingest service (single endpoint)                       │
88	│   POST /v1/capture                                          │
89	│   • validates schema                                        │
90	│   • stores photo in object storage, returns CDN URL         │
91	│   • emits event → intelligence engine                       │
92	│   • fan-out:                                                │
93	│       ├─ if route=whatsapp → WhatsApp Cloud API template    │
94	│       ├─ if route=backend  → Office Ops core                │
95	│       └─ always → audit log + intelligence engine           │
96	└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
97	```
98	
99	### Key design choices
100	
101	**One ingest endpoint, server-side fan-out.** Do *not* let the phone call WhatsApp Cloud API directly. Reasons: WhatsApp tokens would leak onto devices, template approval is centralized, retries and rate-limits need a server. The phone tells the server "route this to WhatsApp" and the server does the WhatsApp call.
102	
103	**The "route" choice is metadata, not two code paths.** The app sends the same JSON regardless; a `destinations: ["whatsapp", "backend"]` field tells the server where to fan out. This keeps the app dumb and the policy on the server, where you can change it without re-releasing the APK.
104	
105	**Photos go to object storage, not embedded in JSON.** The app uploads the photo in the same multipart request, the server replaces it with a CDN URL before fan-out. WhatsApp templates support media; passing a URL is cheaper than re-uploading.
106	
107	**Pre-flight checks run before submit, and their results are signed into the payload.** GPS lat/long, SSID hash, beacon ID, timestamp, device ID — these become tamper-evident evidence on the server side. Don't trust them blindly (a determined user can patch the APK) but log them for audit.
108	
109	**Intelligence engine subscribes to the event stream, never polls the database.** The ingest service emits a `capture.created` event; the intelligence engine consumes it. This decouples the field app from intelligence-engine availability — if the engine is down, captures still land and get processed when it recovers.
110	
111	---
112	
113	## 4. UX considerations for low-literacy users
114	
115	These are platform-independent but easier to enforce in native:
116	
117	- **Icons + colors over text.** Green check, red cross, big camera icon. Where text is needed, use the local language and offer audio playback of the prompt.
118	- **Voice-prompt every screen.** Tap the screen title → it reads aloud. Cheap to add, transformative for adoption.
119	- **One action per screen.** Don't show a form with 8 fields. Scan QR → confirm item with photo of the label → enter quantity with +/- buttons → done.
120	- **Haptic + sound on success.** Workers stop trusting silent confirmations.
121	- **No "submit" word.** Use a big green checkmark button. Use "send" only after the action is irreversible.
122	- **Show queue state plainly.** A badge "3 waiting to send" on the home screen, not buried in settings. When it clears to 0, celebrate it visibly.
123	- **Kiosk / single-app lock option** for site-owned devices, so workers can't accidentally exit to a browser or get stuck in a notification.
124	- **Pre-fill from context.** If the operator scans a QR on a fire extinguisher, the app already knows it's a maintenance task and skips the "what kind of task" question.
125	
126	---
127	
128	## 5. Tech stack suggestion
129	
130	| Layer | Choice | Why |
131	|---|---|---|
132	| App framework | **React Native** (or Flutter if the team prefers Dart) | Native modules for ML Kit, BLE, Wi-Fi; one codebase; large hiring pool |
133	| Local DB | **WatermelonDB** on SQLite | Reactive, scales to 10k+ rows on cheap phones, plays well with sync |
134	| Sync | **WorkManager** via a native module | Survives reboot, OS-managed backoff |
135	| QR/Barcode | **ML Kit** (`@react-native-ml-kit/barcode-scanning`) | Free, on-device, fast |
136	| Photos | `react-native-vision-camera` | Lower-level control than `expo-camera`; needed for compression tuning on cheap devices |
137	| Geofence | `react-native-geolocation-service` + server-side validation | Don't trust the phone alone |
138	| BLE | `react-native-ble-plx` | Mature, beacon support |
139	| Wi-Fi SSID | `react-native-wifi-reborn` | Reads SSID with location permission |
140	| Push | FCM via `@react-native-firebase/messaging` | Reliable, free |
141	| Auth | OIDC + short-lived JWT, refresh on Wi-Fi | Don't ship long-lived secrets to the device |
142	| Distribution | **Private Play track** (preferred) or signed APK + MDM | Auto-updates, rollback |
143	| Crash / telemetry | Sentry + a custom event for "capture sent" success rate | The metric that matters |
144	
145	---
146	
147	## 6. What I would **not** do
148	
149	- **Don't build a PWA first "to learn" and plan to rewrite.** You will not rewrite. The PWA will accrete features and the day you need SSID checks you'll be stuck.
150	- **Don't let the phone hit WhatsApp Cloud API directly.** Token sprawl, no rate-limiting, no audit.
151	- **Don't write your own QR decoder.** ML Kit is free and better than anything you'll ship in a year.
152	- **Don't make the operator choose "WhatsApp or backend" on every submit.** Choose a sensible default per task type on the server, let them override only when needed.
153	- **Don't gate submission on connectivity.** Capture first, sync later. Always.
154	- **Don't try to make iOS work in v1** unless a real customer asks. Every cross-platform compromise is a tax on the 95% case.
155	
156	---
157	
158	## 7. Phased rollout
159	
160	**Phase 1 (4–6 weeks):** Android app with QR scan + photo + form + offline outbox + one ingest endpoint + WhatsApp template fan-out. No BLE, no SSID check yet. Pilot at one site.
161	
162	**Phase 2 (3–4 weeks):** Add GPS geofence, SSID check, signed pre-flight payloads, audit dashboard. Connect to intelligence engine event stream.
163	
164	**Phase 3 (4 weeks):** BLE beacons for indoor location, voice prompts, kiosk mode for site-owned devices, in-app updates.
165	
166	**Phase 4:** iOS if and only if customer demand justifies it.
167	
168	---
169	
170	## 8. Open questions to resolve before coding
171	
172	1. **Who owns the device?** Site-owned (kiosk mode possible, you can pre-provision) vs. operator-owned (BYOD, harder UX, training cost). Changes the answer for distribution and security.
173	2. **WhatsApp account model.** One business number per site, or one central number with site identifier in the template? Affects template approval count and reply routing.
174	3. **What happens to a capture sent to WhatsApp — does anyone *act* on it, or is it informational?** If actionable, the WhatsApp reply needs to flow back into the intelligence engine, which means you need WhatsApp webhook handling on the server.
175	4. **Photo retention policy.** Cheap phones fill up fast. Local cache must auto-evict after server-ack.
176	5. **Language coverage.** Hindi + English minimum? Regional languages? Voice prompts must be pre-recorded per language.
177	6. **What's the failure UX when a pre-flight check fails?** ("You are not on site Wi-Fi" — can they override with a supervisor PIN? Or hard block?)
178	
179	Answer these six before locking the spec.

│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ WorkManager sync worker (exponential backoff)        │   │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
└────────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────┘
                             │ HTTPS (multipart for photos)
                             ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Edge ingest service (single endpoint)                       │
│   POST /v1/capture                                          │
│   • validates schema                                        │
│   • stores photo in object storage, returns CDN URL         │
│   • emits event → intelligence engine                       │
│   • fan-out:                                                │
│       ├─ if route=whatsapp → WhatsApp Cloud API template    │
│       ├─ if route=backend  → Office Ops core                │
│       └─ always → audit log + intelligence engine           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Key design choices

**One ingest endpoint, server-side fan-out.** Do *not* let the phone call WhatsApp Cloud API directly. Reasons: WhatsApp tokens would leak onto devices, template approval is centralized, retries and rate-limits need a server. The phone tells the server "route this to WhatsApp" and the server does the WhatsApp call.

**The "route" choice is metadata, not two code paths.** The app sends the same JSON regardless; a `destinations: ["whatsapp", "backend"]` field tells the server where to fan out. This keeps the app dumb and the policy on the server, where you can change it without re-releasing the APK.

**Photos go to object storage, not embedded in JSON.** The app uploads the photo in the same multipart request, the server replaces it with a CDN URL before fan-out. WhatsApp templates support media; passing a URL is cheaper than re-uploading.

**Pre-flight checks run before submit, and their results are signed into the payload.** GPS lat/long, SSID hash, beacon ID, timestamp, device ID — these become tamper-evident evidence on the server side. Don't trust them blindly (a determined user can patch the APK) but log them for audit.

**Intelligence engine subscribes to the event stream, never polls the database.** The ingest service emits a `capture.created` event; the intelligence engine consumes it. This decouples the field app from intelligence-engine availability — if the engine is down, captures still land and get processed when it recovers.

---

## 4. UX considerations for low-literacy users

These are platform-independent but easier to enforce in native:

- **Icons + colors over text.** Green check, red cross, big camera icon. Where text is needed, use the local language and offer audio playback of the prompt.
- **Voice-prompt every screen.** Tap the screen title → it reads aloud. Cheap to add, transformative for adoption.
- **One action per screen.** Don't show a form with 8 fields. Scan QR → confirm item with photo of the label → enter quantity with +/- buttons → done.
- **Haptic + sound on success.** Workers stop trusting silent confirmations.
- **No "submit" word.** Use a big green checkmark button. Use "send" only after the action is irreversible.
- **Show queue state plainly.** A badge "3 waiting to send" on the home screen, not buried in settings. When it clears to 0, celebrate it visibly.
- **Kiosk / single-app lock option** for site-owned devices, so workers can't accidentally exit to a browser or get stuck in a notification.
- **Pre-fill from context.** If the operator scans a QR on a fire extinguisher, the app already knows it's a maintenance task and skips the "what kind of task" question.

---

## 5. Tech stack suggestion

| Layer | Choice | Why |
|---|---|---|
| App framework | **React Native** (or Flutter if the team prefers Dart) | Native modules for ML Kit, BLE, Wi-Fi; one codebase; large hiring pool |
| Local DB | **WatermelonDB** on SQLite | Reactive, scales to 10k+ rows on cheap phones, plays well with sync |
| Sync | **WorkManager** via a native module | Survives reboot, OS-managed backoff |
| QR/Barcode | **ML Kit** (`@react-native-ml-kit/barcode-scanning`) | Free, on-device, fast |
| Photos | `react-native-vision-camera` | Lower-level control than `expo-camera`; needed for compression tuning on cheap devices |
| Geofence | `react-native-geolocation-service` + server-side validation | Don't trust the phone alone |
| BLE | `react-native-ble-plx` | Mature, beacon support |
| Wi-Fi SSID | `react-native-wifi-reborn` | Reads SSID with location permission |
| Push | FCM via `@react-native-firebase/messaging` | Reliable, free |
| Auth | OIDC + short-lived JWT, refresh on Wi-Fi | Don't ship long-lived secrets to the device |
| Distribution | **Private Play track** (preferred) or signed APK + MDM | Auto-updates, rollback |
| Crash / telemetry | Sentry + a custom event for "capture sent" success rate | The metric that matters |

---

## 6. What I would **not** do

- **Don't build a PWA first "to learn" and plan to rewrite.** You will not rewrite. The PWA will accrete features and the day you need SSID checks you'll be stuck.
- **Don't let the phone hit WhatsApp Cloud API directly.** Token sprawl, no rate-limiting, no audit.
- **Don't write your own QR decoder.** ML Kit is free and better than anything you'll ship in a year.
- **Don't make the operator choose "WhatsApp or backend" on every submit.** Choose a sensible default per task type on the server, let them override only when needed.
- **Don't gate submission on connectivity.** Capture first, sync later. Always.
- **Don't try to make iOS work in v1** unless a real customer asks. Every cross-platform compromise is a tax on the 95% case.

---

## 7. Phased rollout

**Phase 1 (4–6 weeks):** Android app with QR scan + photo + form + offline outbox + one ingest endpoint + WhatsApp template fan-out. No BLE, no SSID check yet. Pilot at one site.

**Phase 2 (3–4 weeks):** Add GPS geofence, SSID check, signed pre-flight payloads, audit dashboard. Connect to intelligence engine event stream.

**Phase 3 (4 weeks):** BLE beacons for indoor location, voice prompts, kiosk mode for site-owned devices, in-app updates.

**Phase 4:** iOS if and only if customer demand justifies it.

---

## 8. Open questions to resolve before coding

1. **Who owns the device?** Site-owned (kiosk mode possible, you can pre-provision) vs. operator-owned (BYOD, harder UX, training cost). Changes the answer for distribution and security.
2. **WhatsApp account model.** One business number per site, or one central number with site identifier in the template? Affects template approval count and reply routing.
3. **What happens to a capture sent to WhatsApp — does anyone *act* on it, or is it informational?** If actionable, the WhatsApp reply needs to flow back into the intelligence engine, which means you need WhatsApp webhook handling on the server.
4. **Photo retention policy.** Cheap phones fill up fast. Local cache must auto-evict after server-ack.
5. **Language coverage.** Hindi + English minimum? Regional languages? Voice prompts must be pre-recorded per language.
6. **What's the failure UX when a pre-flight check fails?** ("You are not on site Wi-Fi" — can they override with a supervisor PIN? Or hard block?)

Answer these six before locking the spec.

201	
202	Start with Phase 1 (scanner + mapping). You can have a guard scanning items at the gate within 3-4 weeks. The bill extraction and AI features layer on top of a working foundation.

├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ EVERY SUBSEQUENT SCAN                                    │
│                                                          │
│  Guard scans: 8901234567890                              │
│       ↓                                                  │
│  App instantly shows:                                    │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────┐                    │
│  │  🧂 Tata Salt 1kg               │                    │
│  │  Expected: 10 units (from order) │                    │
│  │  Scanned so far: 7              │                    │
│  │                                  │                    │
│  │  [+1] [+5] [Custom qty]          │                    │
│  └──────────────────────────────────┘                    │
│                                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!TIP]
> **Accelerator idea**: For the first batch of products, instead of making the guard register each one, extract the product list from DMart bills first. The AI extracts `{name, qty, price}` from the receipt — you pre-populate the product registry. When the guard scans at the gate, most items are already known.

---

## Open Questions for You

1. **Who is the guard?** Is it a dedicated security person, or a rotating staff member? This affects training investment and whether you can use device-owner/kiosk mode.
2. **How many unique products do you typically receive?** If it's 50-100 recurring items, the barcode mapping database fills up in 1-2 weeks. If it's 500+, you may want to consider pre-loading from a public barcode database (like Open Food Facts for grocery items).
3. **DMart bills — are they always printed (thermal receipt), or sometimes handwritten?** This affects whether the server-side AI extraction needs general OCR or can use structured receipt parsing.
4. **Which channels will bill photos come from most often?** Slack, Telegram, admin dashboard upload — or all three? This determines how many ingestion integrations the backend pipeline needs.
5. **Should the AI auto-create orders from bills, or should an admin review/approve first?** Auto-create is faster but risks bad OCR creating wrong orders. Admin review adds a human checkpoint but adds latency.
6. **Is quantity validation per-item (scan each individual item) or per-line-item (scan one, enter count)?** Per-item is more accurate but slower; per-line-item with manual count entry is faster but relies on the guard counting correctly.

---

## Bottom Line

| Path                      | Verdict                                                                                                                                                          |
| ------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **PWA prototype first**   | ❌ Don't do this. Your core workflow (rapid barcode scanning) is the exact thing PWAs are worst at. You'll build it, hate the scanning, then build native anyway. |
| **React Native directly** | ✅**Do this.** The workflows are well-understood, the use case is internal (no product-market-fit risk), and the barcode scanning quality gap is a dealbreaker.   |

Start with Phase 1 (scanner + mapping). You can have a guard scanning items at the gate within 3-4 weeks. The bill extraction and AI features layer on top of a working foundation.
